column2:
Type

(lack of type indicates no type to be used in TEI)

This is a legacy of our early TEI. We keep this information and keep encoding it in new documents for the sake of consistency. It has no searchability or relevance and wasn't implemented rigorously. What I mean is that categories and labels aren't very good, and because of the sprawl of annotations did not end up being applied consistently.
column4:
Name

This is the title/label of the one liner and should match the text preceding the colon in the write-up itself.
column5:
Write-Up

The text preceding the colon in this column should match the text in the name/label column.
column6:
Sources & Notes

Wherever possible, the information in this column should be in the format of a citation rather than a single link. Full/formal citations are not necessary, but the goal should always be to allow someone else to follow the documentary/digital trail back to the item, whether or not its digital location has changed.

column5: Aleuts: Native maritime people of the Aleutian Islands, in southwest Alaska. Much of their land came under the control of the Russian Empire in the mid-eighteenth century. The Aleuts, or Unangax, like the Natives of Kodiak Island, were pressed into service hunting maritime animals for the Russian America Company in the nineteenth century; it is likely, however, that hunters from Kodiak Island, not the Aleutian Island chain, were involved in the 1814 massacre of the Nicoleños. During the nineteenth century, many Aleuts were forced into labor at seasonal seal-hunting camps on the remote Pribilof Islands of St. George and St. Paul. Abusive conditions continued with commercial fishing on these islands during the American era. Today, the Aleutian Pribilof Islands Association is a federally recognized tribe with government-to-government relations with the United States.
column6: ref #1814huntingparty

column5: American Fur Company: a fur trading company founded in 1808 by John J. Astor. The American Fur Company dominated trade in the Great Lakes region and competed with the Hudson's Bay Company in the Pacific Northwest. Astor's trading post on the Columbia River was contracted to supply Russian Alaska with food prior to the War of 1812.
column6: http://www.encyclopedia.com/history/united-states-and-canada/us-history/american-fur-company

column5: American Museum: a museum opened by P. T. Barnum in Manhattan from 1841-65. Its exhibits attracted members of various classes and featured natural history specimens, paintings, wax figures, memorabilia, and events such as lectures. The museum was destroyed in a fire on July 13, 1865, leading to the creation of a circus that ran for nearly 150 years.
column6:
http://www.loc.gov/exhibits/treasures/tri154.html
http://lostmuseum.cuny.edu/archive/barnums-american-museum

column5: Aztec: the Native people of northern Mesoamerica, who ruled a large, complex empire from the mid-fourteenth to the early sixteenth centuries. The empire was destroyed when the army of Spanish conquistador Hernán Cortés captured its capital city of Tenochtitlán (present-day Mexico City) in 1521. At its height, the Aztec Empire had a population of eleven million people who were supported by an elaborate agriculture system. The civilization is renowned today for its magnificent art and architecture, including its religious temples, and for its codices and calendars.
column6:
https://www.britannica.com/topic/Aztec http://www.ancient.eu/Aztec_Civilization/
https://www.britannica.com/place/Tenochtitlan http://www.houstonculture.org/mexico/mexico2.html

column5: Barbareños: Chumash people who are associated with Mission Santa Barbara and who, before the mission era, lived along the Pacific coast in the region between Point Conception and Rincon Point.

Boardman and Pope: a Boston-based firm engaged in the Northwest and China trade during the early decades of the nineteenth century. The shipping enterprise has been falsely connected with the massacre of Nicoleños by Russian American Company hunters. Various periodical accounts stated that the captain of a Boardman & Pope ship, the Charon, left a hunting group on San Nicolas Island in 1811, and the hunters killed the Nicoleños. Russian documents show that the massacre did not involve the Charon or its captain; moreover, the battle took place in 1814.

Bryant & Sturgis: a Boston firm that traded in California throughout the Mexican period. Bryant & Sturgis purchased hides, tallow, and local produce from the ranches and missions, selling or exchanging them for manufactured goods.

Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA): an agency of the US federal government established in 1824 to administer the nation’s trade and treaty relations with Native tribes within US borders (prior to this date, various configurations were used to manage government-to-government relationships between the United States and Native nations). The BIA became the entity responsible for distributing aid to the newly dislocated Native peoples when removed from their homelands and relocated to remote reservations during the nineteenth century. During the era in which assimilation dominated Native policy, the BIA administered the privatization of communal lands through an allotment program and ran boarding schools designed to educate Native youth away from tribal identities. Today, the Bureau of Indian Affairs is housed in the Department of the Interior and has a mission to support tribes’ self-determination.

California Missions: the 21 Catholic missions in Alta California, built between 1769-1823, initially under the leadership of the Spanish Franciscan Father Junípero Serra. The mission system was designed to both Christianize and control the Native population and to establish Spain's political and economic dominion over the region (which would eventually become the US state of California). Missions stretched along El Camino Real, a coastal road, from San Diego in the South to San Francisco in the North; each was placed about a day's drive from each other. Following Mexican independence, the missions were secularized, in 1833, and mission properties were sold to private interests.
column6: http://www.seecalifornia.com/missions/directory.html

column5: California Yacht Club: a social organization for yacht owners in the Los Angeles area. The club was founded in 1922 and located at Wilmington Harbor; in 1961, it relocated to Marina del Rey.
column6:
http://blogs.dailybreeze.com/history/2013/12/01/the-california-yacht-club-in-wilmington/
http://www.calyachtclub.com/club

column5: Catalina Yacht Club: one of the oldest West Coast yacht clubs that has operated continually (except during World War II) since 1924. Catalina’s owners, the Banning Brothers, founded the yacht club in 1892 but it never flourished and was disbanded in 1914. The club reopened in 1924 when James Jump signed a lease with the Santa Catalina Island Company and re-founded the club, which promotes nautical hobbies such as sailing and fishing for sport.
column6: "Catalina Island Yacht Club." Islapedia. Accessed January 29, 2017. http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=Catalina_Island_Yacht_Club.

column5: Chumash: Native people whose homeland spans the coast from Morro Bay to Malibu and includes the northern California Channel Islands. The Chumash are likely the first Natives to have interacted with the Lone Woman when she arrived in Santa Barbara in 1853. In fact, two Santa Barbara Chumash men, Malquiares and Policarpio, were members of the hunting party, led by George Nidever, that brought the Lone Woman to the mainland. Some bands of Chumash people gained federal recognition in the late twentieth century, and today there is one Chumash reservation: Santa Ynez Indian Reservation. Many Chumash are engaged in cultural revitalization, including the teaching and study of the Chumash language and communal building and paddling of the tomol associated with their maritime culture.

Creoles (Russian America): people of mixed ethnic ancestry, for example, of a Russian father and Alaska Native mother or Creole father and Alaska Native mother.  As outlined in the Russian American Company charter of 1821, Creoles constituted a separate estate within the Russian Empire, and they played a prominent role in the commercial activities of the RAC.  Creoles typically spoke Russian and were educated within the colony and/or in the mother country alongside Russians.  Within the fur trade, their work mirrored that of Russians more closely than that of Natives.  By the mid nineteenth century, the number of Creoles within Alaskan towns such as Sitka nearly equaled the number of Russian settlers.
column6: Ilya Vinkovetsky, Russian America: An Overseas Colony of a Continental Empire, 1804-1867 (Oxford UP,2011), 142-49

column5: Daughters of the American Revolution: an organization founded in 1890 for female descendants of soldiers who fought in the American Revolution. Members, who must prove that they are a direct descendent of a Revolutionary War soldier, promote education and historical preservation. During much of the twentieth century, the DAR was infamous for its racism and, in 1939, then First Lady Eleanor Roosevelt renounced her membership after the national organization barred the renowned African-American singer Marian Anderson from performing in their constitutional Hall in Washington, DC. In the twenty-first century, however, the organization has worked to cultivate membership diversity.
column6: "Who We Are." DAR: Daughters of the American Revolution. November 08, 2016. Accessed January 24, 2017. http://www.dar.org/national-society/about-dar/who-we-are/who-we-are.

column5: Digger Indians: a derogatory term initially used by American miners to describe the Native people of central California, who obtained a substantial portion of their caloric needs through indigenous vegetation, including acorns and roots.
Fernandeños: Gabrieliño (Tongva) peoples who lived at Mission San Fernando, south of Santa Barbara, California.

Gabrielinos: Native peoples of the Los Angeles Basin and the southern Channel Islands. After the arrival of the Spanish and establishment of the mission system, they acquired the name Gabrielino due to their residence at Mission San Gabriel. Today, some Gabrielino call themselves Gabrielino while others use the terms Tongva or Kizh instead of or alongside the name Gabrielino. Twenty-first century Gabrielino (Tongva) people are striving to gain federal recognition as a tribe with the right to maintain government-to-government relations with the United States.

Inuit: indigenous people of the North American Arctic, including parts of the United States (Alaska), Canada, and Greenland. The name derives from the Inukitut word for "people." The name Eskimo, which is associated with the colonial past, was previously used by outsiders to describe both the Inuit and Yupik peoples of Alaska, who speak languages that belong to the same linguistic group.  The Alutiiq (Sugpiaq) people, including those of Kodiak Island, have at times been misidentified as "Inuit;" they are Pacific Yupik.
column6: https://www.uaf.edu/anlc/resources/inuit-eskimo/

column5:
Josiah Marshall (1773-1841): a businessman based in Boston, Massachusetts and co-owner, with Dixey Wildes, of the Marshall & Wildes shipping business.

Dixey Wildes: partner with Josiah Marshall in the Boston-based Marshall & Wildes shipping firm, which was engaged in trade with China.
Kanakas: a derogatory term used by Europeans and Americans in the nineteenth century to designate Hawaiian Islanders.

Kashaya Pomo: California people whose homeland is north of San Francisco Bay in coastal Sonoma County. During the nineteenth century, some Kashaya Pomo were recruited by the Russian American Company to work as seasonal laborers at Fort Ross. The Kashaya Pomo chose to form an alliance with the Russians in part to protect themselves against Spanish missionary efforts and incursions by Mexican colonists. Members of the Kashaya Pomo community were recruited to play the people of Karana’s community, as well as the Aleuts, in the 1964 film adaptation of Island of the Blue Dolphins.  Today, the Kashia Band of Pomo Indians of the Stewarts Point Rancheria is a federally recognized tribe.

Kodiak Alutiiq (Sugpiaq): Native maritime people of Kodiak Island.  Their territory came under the control of the Russian Empire in 1784 when a permanent Russian settlement was established on Kodiak Island. The Russians often referred to the Kodiak Alutiit as "Aleuts," but they are a linguistically and culturally distinct people from those who live on the Aleutian Islands. During the early nineteenth century, the Kodiak Alutiit, who were expert marine mammal hunters, were forced to hunt fur-bearing animals for the government-sponsored Russian American Company. Sailing ships brought Russian overseers and Kodiak islanders to hunt otters on San Nicolas and other California Channel Islands. When Alaska was purchased by the United States, the Kodiak Alutiit confronted new pressures of assimilation. The 1971 Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act brought significant benefits to Alutiiq villages; today, cultural revitalization efforts are flourishing on Kodiak Island.
column6: ref #1814huntingparty

column5: La Purisima Mission: a Spanish mission established in the Chumash village of Algsacupi (present-day Lompoc) founded in 1787. Also called La Purísima Concepción, in 1804, at the mission’s peak, 1,520 Chumash neophytes lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1874. It is now a California State Historic Park and serves as a living history museum.

Los Angeles Mission: a Catholic mission located in present-day Los Angeles, California and founded in 1797, although the mission's first church was not completed until 1799. Also known as San Fernando Mission or San Fernando Rey de España, by 1811 the mission had 1,081 residents, most of whom were Tataviam or Gabrielino (Tongva). The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro in 1835 were not brought to this mission due to its secularization in 1834; instead, some of the group were baptized at the Los Angeles Plaza Church. The mission was returned to the Catholic Church in 1861.

Luiseños: Native people of southern California whose homeland extends from southern Los Angeles County to northern San Diego County. They acquired the name Luiseños from their association with Mission San Luis Rey in present-day Oceanside, California. As of 2016, six bands of Luiseño people had been federally recognized as tribes with government-to-government relations with the United States, including the Pechanga Band, which in 2015 submitted a repatriation claim to the US Navy citing cultural affiliation with the Nicoleños and requesting a return of all funerary and cultural objects held by the Navy on San Nicolas Island. The claim was accepted. Linguists have determined that the Luiseño language came from the Cupan group of the Takic branch of the Uto-Aztecan language, and some believe the four recorded words spoken by the Lone Woman resemble the Luiseño language more closely than any other.

Mission Fathers: a term applied collectively, in California, to the Franciscan priests who oversaw the twenty-one Catholic missions established between 1769 and 1833. The missions sought to "gather in" the Native peoples of California, converting them to Christianity and European lifeways. No evidence documenting a priest's request to search for the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island has been found to this date.

Mission Indians: unidentified California Natives who were associated with one or more of the twenty-one Catholic missions in California. In many European-American documents, they are referred to as Christian neophytes, with their tribal identities omitted. In only some instances can historical research uncover tribal or individual identities of those referenced.

Musée de l’Homm: a museum in Paris, France, established in 1938. The museum’s precursor was the Musée d’Ethnographie du Trocadéro, established in 1882 and in operation until it was joined with the Musée de Sculpture Comparée to form the Musée de l’Homme. French geologist and ethnographer Léon de Cessac collected artifacts from San Nicolas Island for this museum in the 1870s; they are still held in the museum’s collections today.
column6: "From the Palais du Trocadéro to the Palais de Chaillot : 1882-2015." Musée de l'Homme. Accessed February 08, 2017. http://www.museedelhomme.fr/en/museum/history-musee-homme/palais-trocadero-palais-chaillot-1882-2015.

column5: Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County: a museum featuring exhibits about both natural history and culture. Opened in 1913 as the Los Angeles County Museum of History, Science, and Art, the museum was part of a strategy to turn local fairgrounds into a cultural center. The state of California built an exposition building, the county built the museum, and the city agreed to maintain the grounds. The museum was renovated in 2007-09 to restore the original designs while also modernizing the structure.
column6: "History of the Natural History Museum." Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County. Accessed January 28, 2017. http://www.nhm.org/site/about-our-museums/history.

column5: Nicoleños: the people of San Nicolas Island. Although they are Island Gabrielinos (Tongva), they became known as Nicoleños because of the Spanish name given to the island. In the early nineteenth century, the Nicoleño population dropped following contact and conflicts with hunters from Europe, North America, Alaska, and Hawaii. All the Nicoleños, except the Lone Woman, were taken from the island in 1835 on the ship Peor es Nada and brought to San Pedro, near Los Angeles, California.
Pepimaros: a term used to describe Native peoples on the mainland who had migrated to California from the Channel Islands.

Pierce-Arrow Motor Car Company: a manufacturer of luxury cars that operated from 1870-1938. Founder George N. Pierce began the business as a bicycle manufacturer, but, after producing his first single cylinder automobile in 1901, renamed the company and began producing cars full time. In 1928, the company was sold to an Indiana automobile company named Studebaker, but the Great Depression strongly affected business. The company filed for bankruptcy in 1938.
column6: "Pierce Arrow History." Pierce-Arrow Museum. Accessed January 29, 2017. http://www.pierce-arrow.com/history.

column4: University of California Robert H. Lowie Museum of Anthropology
column5: Robert H. Lowie Museum of Anthropology: the University of California’s museum of anthropology founded in 1901 and named after Berkeley professor and anthropologist Dr. Robert H. Lowie. In 1991, it was renamed the Phoebe A. Hurst Museum of Anthropology after its philanthropist founder. Ishi and Alfred Kroeber, the man who studied him, are perhaps the most famous figures associated with the museum. A Yahi man who was deemed "the last of his tribe," Ishi lived at the museum more or less as an exhibit from 1911 until his death from tuberculosis in 1916.
column6: "History of the Museum." Phoebe A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology. Accessed January 28, 2017. http://hearstmuseum.berkeley.edu/about/history-of-museum.Kroeber, Karl, and Clifton Kroeber, eds. Ishi in Three Centuries. University of Nebraska, 2003.

column5: Rogers Brothers: refers to Eugene Frederick, Alvah, and Herbert Augustine Rogers, the sons of Augustine B. and Hannah E. Perkins Rogers. The eldest son, Eugene, founded a seal and sea otter hunting and abalone fishing operation out of Santa Barbara in 1875. In addition to employing his younger brothers, he hired Chinese abalone fishermen and two of George Nidever's sons, George and Jake.
column6: Islapedia: http://islapedia.com/index.php?title=ROGERS_BROTHERS

column5: Russian American Company: a joint-stock company founded in 1799 and given a monopoly on fur hunting and trade in Russian Alaska. For much of its history, the RAC was headquartered in Sitka (then New Archangel), Alaska. It served as the administrative center of the Russian Empire in North America.

San Buenaventura Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1782 and located near the Chumash village of Mitsquanaqa'n in the city of Ventura, California. At the mission's peak, in 1816, 1,328 neophytes, mostly Chumash people, lived here. In 1818, the mission was evacuated for a month due to the threat of a pirate attack. The mission was secularized in 1836 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862.

San Diego Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1769 and located in San Diego, California. Also called San Diego de Alcalá, in 1824, at the mission’s peak, there were 1,829 neophytes, primarily Tipai-Ipai (Kumeyaay), living at the mission, though a food shortage allowed the Tipai-Ipai (Kumeyaay) to remain in their villages. San Diego Mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862. Descendants of the Mission Indians later became known as Digueños, after the mission.

San Fernando Mission: a Catholic mission founded in 1797 and located in present-day Los Angeles, California, although the mission’s first church was not completed until 1799. Also called San Fernando Rey de de España and Los Angeles Mission, by 1811 the mission had 1,081 residents, most of whom were Tataviam or Gabrielino (Tongva). The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro in 1835 were not brought to this mission due to its secularization in 1834; instead, some of the group were baptized at the Los Angeles Plaza Church. The mission was returned to the Catholic Church in 1861.

San Francisco Mercantile Library: a library founded by the Mercantile Library Association of San Francisco. By 1874, the institution had around 36,000 volumes. The library merged with the collection of the San Francisco Mechanics’ Institute, but the combined library was destroyed in the 1906 earthquake. A new Mechanics’-Mercantile Library opened in 1910.

San Francisco Mission: a Catholic mission located in San Francisco, California, that was founded in 1776. Also known as the Mission Delores or San Fransicso de Asís, between 1801-21, at the mission’s height, 1000-1200 neophytes, mainly Ohlone, Bay Miwok, Coast Miwok, and Patwin, lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1857.

San Gabriel Mission: a Catholic mission located nine miles east of downtown Los Angeles, California, and was founded in 1771. Also called San Gabriel Arcángel, at the mission’s peak, in 1817, 1,701 Gabrielino (Tongva) neophytes lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Church in 1859 via a proclamation signed by President James Buchanan. The Nicoleños who arrived in San Pedro, California, in 1835 were not brought to San Gabriel Mission, which was then secularized after Mexican independence. Those men and women for whom records have been found remained in the Los Angeles area.

San Luis Obispo: the fifth Catholic mission in California, located in a coastal valley the Spanish called the "Valley of the Bears." Founded in 1772, this was the first mission established on the land of the Chumash, and it had a record population of 832 people in 1804. The mission was secularized in 1835 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1859.

San Ynez Mission: a Catholic mission located in the Santa Inéz Valley on the eastern edge of Solvang, California. Also called Santa Inés Virgen y Martír or simply Santa Inés, it was founded in 1804 and, at the mission's peak in 1815, 768 neophytes, mostly Chumash, lived here. The largest Native uprising during the mission era began here in 1824. The mission was secularized in 1836 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1862.

Santa Barbara Mission: a Catholic mission located in Santa Barbara, California, that was founded in 1786 near the Chumash village of Xana'yan. It was the tenth mission founded by the Franciscans in California and is known as the "Queen of the Missions" because of its beauty. In 1803, at the mission's peak, 1,792 neophytes, Chumash peoples referred to as the Barbareño and the Canaleño, lived here. The mission was secularized in 1834 and returned to the Catholic Church in 1865. In 1853, the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island was conditionally baptized and buried at the mission. She was not reunited with her people at the mission, as they had been brought to Los Angeles. Today the Santa Barbara Mission is owned and operated by the Franciscan order, serving as both a church and a museum.

Santa Cruz Island Company: the owners of Santa Cruz Island between 1869-87. Ten investors formed the company after purchasing the island from William Barron in the late nineteenth century; the company then operated a cattle and sheep ranch on the island. In the 1880s, one shareholder, Justinian Caire, bought out his partners. The land was divided among his family members, but most was then sold to Edwin Stanton by 1937. Stanton's son, Carey Stanton, left most of the island to the Santa Cruz Island Foundation, which operates the Nature Conservancy, in 1987.
column6: United States. National Park Service. "Santa Cruz Island History and Culture." National Parks Service. Accessed January 29, 2017. https://www.nps.gov/chis/learn/historyculture/santacruzisland.htm.

column5: Serra Shop: the gift store at Santa Barbara Mission.

The Tuna Club: a private big-game fishing organization on Catalina Island, California. Charles Frederick Holder founded the club in 1898, wanting to promote stricter rules for anglers as well as marine life conservation. The club, still in operation today, has counted many celebrities and US presidents among its members. Its 1908 clubhouse is now a US National Historic Landmark.
column6: http://www.islapedia.com/index.php?title=Tuna_Club,_Avalon,_Santa_Catalina_Island http://www.latimes.com/travel/deals/la-trb-tuna-club-tours-20150730-post.html https://www.igfa.org/Museum/HOF-Holder.aspx

column5: Toltecs: a civilization that prospered in central Mexico from the tenth to twelfth centuries. The Toltecs crafted monumental pyramids and statues that reflected the overall militaristic nature of their empire. The Aztecs—who rose to power several centuries later—claimed descent from the Toltecs, whom they greatly admired. The Toltecs capital, Tollan, was located about fifty miles north of present-day Mexico City.
column6:
http://www.ancient.eu/Toltec_Civilization/ 
https://www.britannica.com/topic/Toltec

column5: Vatican: a sovereign city-state that was part of the city of Rome during the mid-nineteenth century. The Vatican is home to the Vatican Museum, a collection of art and artifacts collected by Catholic popes beginning in the sixteenth century and organized into separate galleries and museums in the eighteenth century. The Museum of Propaganda, one of those collections, was established to highlight the missionary achievements of evangelizers in the New World. It is currently unclear whether objects associated with the Lone Woman ever reached Rome and the Vatican Museum.
Ventureños: Chumash peoples associated with Mission San Buenaventura; prior to the mission era, they lived along the Pacific coast in the region stretching from Carpenteria to Malibu, California.
